title: A Day as a Trauma Surgeon
tagline: Three patients, one critical, sixteen hours to get it right.

wake_up.text: Your alarm blares. Somewhere in the dark, your pager is already glowing — a text from the night resident: "Busy one. See you at handoff." Today you're covering three cases. One of them is critical.

choices.text: Hit snooze for 15 more minutes
Get up immediately and prep your bag

late_start.text: You're moving fast now, coffee in one hand, badge in the other. You'll make it — barely.

choices.text: Keep moving

commute.text: Empty roads. The hospital lights up ahead like a lighthouse. You have a few minutes to spare before handoff.

choices.text: Stop for real coffee, not the break-room sludge
Skip it and go straight in early

arrive_hospital.text: Night shift hands you the board: a hernia repair, a gallbladder, and a motorcycle-accident admit with internal bleeding they've stabilized overnight. That last one is yours first.

choices.text: Read the full overnight chart before rounds
Get the two-minute verbal summary and move on

morning_rounds.text: You check in on the motorcycle patient, stable but pale, and the two elective cases waiting downstairs. The board says you're in OR 3 by 08:30.

choices.text: Sit with the trauma patient for a minute before scrubbing in
Head straight to the OR to prep

or_prep.text: Gloves on, mask up. The room is cold and quiet, the way it always is right before it isn't. Your resident checks the monitors one last time.

choices.text: Begin the procedure

surgery_start.text: Forty minutes in, the nurse's voice cuts through the room: "BP's dropping — 88 over 52 and falling." Every second you spend deciding is a second the patient doesn't have.

choices.text: Continue — stopping now is riskier than pushing through
Pause and stabilize BP yourself before continuing
Ask the anesthesiologist to manage it while you keep working
Call in the senior surgeon on backup

surgery_outcome_continue.text: You push through. It's close — closer than you'd like — but the bleeding was where you expected it, and the numbers climb back on their own right as you close. It worked. This time.

choices.text: Finish up and scrub out

surgery_outcome_stabilize.text: You take the extra six minutes. The room tenses, but the numbers steady, and when you go back in, everything is exactly where you left it. Textbook.

choices.text: Finish up and scrub out

surgery_outcome_team.text: The anesthesiologist works the numbers while your hands stay exactly where they need to be. It's the kind of moment that reminds you why you trust this team.

choices.text: Finish up and scrub out

surgery_outcome_senior.text: Backup arrives in four minutes flat. Between the two of you, the case wraps clean — but you catch the look the resident gives you. Some of them will remember you called for help on a routine drop.

choices.text: Finish up and scrub out

lunch_break.text: Two cases down, one to go. You have maybe twenty real minutes before the pager goes off again.

choices.text: Sit down and actually eat
Skip it and get ahead on paperwork

afternoon_call.text: Trauma page: multi-car accident inbound, ETA six minutes. Two patients, one described as "unstable." Your third scheduled case will have to wait.

choices.text: Take point on the unstable patient yourself
Assign your resident to lead under your supervision

equipment_failure.text: Mid-assessment, the portable monitor glitches and drops the readout entirely. The tech line is backed up for another eight minutes.

choices.text: Switch to manual vitals and keep going
Hold the assessment and wait for a working unit

family_conversation.text: The patient's family arrives, frightened and talking over each other in the hallway. Someone has to walk them through what happens next.

choices.text: Be direct about the risks, plainly and without softening it
Focus on what's going right and keep the tone hopeful

evening_rounds.text: One last pass through all three patients. Vitals hold. The hallway is quieter now than it's been all day.

choices.text: Head to handoff

handoff_night_shift.text: The night resident is waiting with a coffee and a fresh chart. Fourteen hours in, this is the last thing standing between you and the parking lot.

choices.text: Walk through every case in full detail
Give the short version and trust them to ask questions

end_shift.text: The board is clear. Whatever today was, it's over now.

choices.text: Clock out

triumphant.title: Hero of the Shift
triumphant.blurb: Every call held. The department is already talking about it — calmly, confidently, the way you like it.

steady_hand.title: Steady Hand
steady_hand.blurb: Nothing flashy. Nothing missed. The kind of day that quietly builds a reputation.

ordinary_day.title: Ordinary Day
ordinary_day.blurb: A normal, forgettable Tuesday in trauma surgery — which, in this job, is its own kind of win.

burned_out.title: Running on Empty
burned_out.blurb: You made it to the parking lot, but there is nothing left in the tank. Tomorrow needs to look different.

written_up.title: Under Review
written_up.blurb: A few calls today are going to come up in tomorrow's case review. Nobody was hurt — but questions are coming.
